entry.rationale: The 1880 U.S. Census is the first federal enumeration to record each person's relationship to the household head, making it the highest-priority remaining source for direct relationship evidence. If Thomas Flynn and Patrick Flynn appear in the same household, Patrick's entry would carry 'Son' in the relationship column — direct evidence unavailable in the 1850 and 1860 censuses already searched. Even if Patrick (age ~35) heads his own household, enumerating Thomas's household separately may place him among named children. Both Thomas Flynn (age ~62) and Patrick Flynn (age ~35) should be searched simultaneously; results prime the sibling roster for the FAN steps that follow.

The 1870 census fills the gap between the 1860 household already searched and the 1880 relationship-column census. Thomas Flynn (~52) should appear in Schuylkill County. His 1870 household reveals which children remained at home after Patrick's departure, providing names for the sibling FAN cluster. Those siblings, once identified, become targets for their own later records — death certificates, marriage records, church sacraments — that would independently corroborate Thomas Flynn as their common father.

Per the record-type guide, a parentage plan requires a dedicated search for the parents' marriage record. Thomas Flynn's marriage to Patrick's mother: (a) supplies the mother's maiden name, which no source found so far has provided; (b) confirms Thomas and his wife as a couple before Patrick's ca. 1845 birth; and (c) corroborates fatherhood otherwise shown only by indirect census evidence and a secondary informant on Patrick's death certificate. Patrick was born in Ireland (~1845), so the original marriage may predate emigration — but if Thomas married or remarried in Pennsylvania ca. 1846–1855, a county marriage bond or church register entry would capture it. FamilySearch collection 1401638 ('Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985') covers this period.

Patrick Flynn married ca. 1865–1875, as evidenced by his son-in-law James Brown serving as informant on the 1908 death certificate. Marriage records — especially Catholic church registers — routinely name both sets of parents. Even if no parents are listed, the witnesses are almost always close relatives or neighbors. Identifying those witnesses opens additional FAN leads: a witness sharing the Flynn surname is a probable sibling; a neighbor-witness who also appears in the 1850–1860 Thomas Flynn household corroborates the community network surrounding the family.

Schuylkill County's Irish immigrant community was overwhelmingly Catholic, attending parishes including St. Patrick's (Pottsville, est. 1827), St. Joseph's (Minersville), and St. Mary's (Shenandoah). FamilySearch collection 1401638 ('Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985') indexes baptismal, marriage, and burial registers from these parishes. Catholic marriage registers name both sets of parents; baptismal registers record parents and godparents — godparents are a prime FAN source, typically siblings or close community members of the parents. Any Flynn children born after the family's arrival (~1847 onward) would appear under 'Thomas Flynn and [wife]' as parents, directly corroborating the parentage pattern. This item is also the fallback for the Thomas Flynn civil marriage search (pli_009): if no county marriage record is found, the Catholic register is the next most likely location.

Other children of Thomas Flynn who died in Pennsylvania after 1906 — when statewide mandatory death registration began — would have death certificates naming 'Thomas Flynn' as father. This is the core FAN-cluster technique: tracing Patrick's siblings through their own independent records to corroborate the common ancestor. Siblings to trace will be identified from the 1870 and 1880 Thomas Flynn household searches (pli_007, pli_008). Multiple siblings' death certificates naming the same father constitute independent, corroborating lines of evidence distinct from the single secondary-informant statement already in hand. Pennsylvania death certificates from 1906 onward are indexed on FamilySearch and fully name both parents.

Thomas Flynn, born Ireland ca. 1818, would have sought naturalization in Schuylkill County. Pre-1906 naturalizations were filed in county courts — Schuylkill County's Quarter Sessions or Court of Common Pleas both had jurisdiction. FamilySearch indexes Pennsylvania naturalization records for this period. While pre-1906 petitions vary in family detail, they sometimes list the applicant's wife and children by name; at minimum, confirming Thomas's Irish origin and Schuylkill County residence supports the correct identification of Thomas as Patrick's father rather than a namesake.

I have the full project context. Let me review what's known before writing:

- **q_001** (Who were Patrick's parents?) is `in_progress` — the primary open question
- **pl_002** for q_001 is `completed` — all 3 items done (1860 census, death cert, probate)
- Next plan IDs: **pl_003** (plan), **pli_007–pli_013** (items)
- Patrick was born ~1845, Ireland; found with Thomas Flynn in both the 1850 and 1860 censuses; death certificate (secondary informant) names Thomas as father; probate search was negative
- **Critical gap noted in ps_001:** 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses not yet searched

This is **add-new mode** — the prior plan is completed and the question remains unproved. I'll do a quick collections check to confirm census availability before writing the plan.The collections search confirms FamilySearch has Pennsylvania probate (already searched) and church/town records at the state level — US Federal Census collections are national-scope, so they'll appear via search rather than this scope filter. From established knowledge, all three target years (1870, 1880, 1900) are fully indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry; importantly, **1880 is the first census with an explicit "relationship to head" column**, making it the highest-value search for this parentage question. The 1890 census was nearly entirely destroyed in a 1921 fire and will be omitted.

Now I'll write the new plan as one batched call — pl_003 with items pli_007 through pli_013.Plan **pl_003** is written — 7 items, all `planned`. Here's the full picture:

---

## Plan pl_003 — Post-1860 Federal Censuses for q_001
**Question:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, Ireland, d. 1908)?

---

### Step 1 — pli_007 · 1870 Census, Patrick Flynn · Schuylkill County, PA · FamilySearch
Patrick would be approximately 25 — old enough to head his own household or remain with Thomas (~52). This establishes his whereabouts in the gap between 1860 and death, and tests whether the co-residence pattern from 1850 and 1860 continued into 1870. ⚠️ *The 1870 census does not state family relationships — that column was introduced in 1880.*

### Step 2 — pli_008 · 1870 Census, Thomas Flynn · Schuylkill County, PA · FamilySearch
*(Execute only if Thomas is **not** co-resident in pli_007.)* If Thomas has his own household by 1870, finding him independently confirms he was alive post-1860 and narrows the death-date window — critical context given the failed probate search (pli_006 was negative for 1870–1900). May also surface other Flynn children who corroborate the family structure.

### Step 3 — pli_009 · 1880 Census, Patrick Flynn · Schuylkill County, PA · FamilySearch ⭐
**Highest-value search in the plan.** The 1880 census is the **first federal census with an explicit "Relationship to Head of Family" column.** If Thomas (~62) is still living and shares Patrick's household, the relationship column will directly state the parent-child connection — elevating what has so far been *inferred* indirect evidence to *recorded* explicit evidence. Even if Thomas is absent, the 1880 household reveals Patrick's occupation, adult children, and any Flynn siblings.

### Step 4 — pli_010 · 1880 Census, Thomas Flynn · Schuylkill County, PA · FamilySearch
*(Execute only if Thomas is **not** co-resident in pli_009.)* Thomas would be approximately 62 in 1880 — plausibly alive. If found in another child's household, the 1880 relationship column will name him as "father" or similar to that child, further corroborating the Flynn family structure. Failure to find him in both 1870 and 1880 suggests he died between 1860 and 1870 — pointing toward non-Schuylkill death records or vital records in that window.

### Step 5 — pli_011 · 1900 Census, Patrick Flynn · Schuylkill County, PA · FamilySearch
Patrick died in 1908 and must appear in 1900 (~55). Anchors his life across the 1860–1908 gap, confirms continued Schuylkill County residence, and reveals household members — adult children or others whose ages and birthplaces may corroborate the family timeline. The 1900 schedule also records years married and mother's country of origin, which could yield additional parentage clues. *(Note: The 1890 census is excluded — nearly entirely destroyed in a 1921 fire.)*

### Step 6 — pli_012 · 1870 Census, Pennsylvania · Ancestry · *fallback for pli_007*
*(Execute only if pli_007 yields nothing.)* Ancestry's independent index catches surname variants (Flynn / Flinn / Flyn) that the FamilySearch index may miss, and the broader Pennsylvania scope accommodates a possible move to an adjacent county.

### Step 7 — pli_013 · 1880 Census, Pennsylvania · Ancestry · *fallback for pli_009*
*(Execute only if pli_009 yields nothing.)* Because the 1880 census is the only census capable of supplying explicit relationship evidence, an independent cross-check is especially warranted here. Different indexing workflows mean Ancestry may surface what FamilySearch missed.

---

### Scope summary
| Step | Year | Subject | Repository | Condition |
|------|------|---------|-----------|-----------|
| pli_007 | 1870 | Patrick Flynn | FamilySearch | Always |

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn died in 1908 in Schuylkill County — within Pennsylvania's statewide death registration period (began 1906). The FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968' is indexed and freely accessible. The certificate's 'parents' field, if completed by a knowledgeable informant (surviving spouse or child), directly names both father and mother — making this the highest-yield single source for the parentage question. A blank or uncertain parents field is itself a negative data point worth recording.

The 1850 census placed Patrick Flynn (age 5) in Thomas Flynn's household — indirect evidence only, since 1850 does not state relationships. Tracking this household across subsequent censuses substantially strengthens or weakens the parentage hypothesis. In 1860 Patrick (~15) was likely still at home; the 1860 return will also show any wife of Thomas Flynn by name (the 1850 household notes do not record a wife, or her name was not captured). The 1880 census is particularly important: it introduced the relationship-to-head column, which would state 'son' or 'father' explicitly. The 1900 census (Patrick ~55) may give his parents' birthplaces. All four decades are indexed on FamilySearch at no cost. Note: the 1890 census is largely destroyed — bridge between 1880 and 1900.

If Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) died in Schuylkill County, his will or administration in the county Orphans' Court would name his heirs. A will that names 'my son Patrick Flynn' is direct evidence of the parent-child relationship — the strongest possible corroboration for the 1850 census co-enumeration hypothesis. The FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' covers Schuylkill County and is indexed. Search under Thomas Flynn; also search variant spellings (Flyn, Flinn). Thomas could have died anytime from the 1860s onward, so the full range 1860-1910 should be checked.

Fallback for the indexed probate search (sequence 3). FamilySearch volume 007936749 contains 412 browse-only probate images for Schuylkill County, 1851-1930, with 0% indexed. If Thomas Flynn does not surface in the indexed search, these images must be browsed manually — likely organized by decedent surname within the county docket books. Browse this volume if the indexed search returns no result.

A dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn and his wife is required in every parentage plan. Pennsylvania statewide civil marriage registration did not begin until 1913; the county marriage bond/license series for Schuylkill County starts too late for a marriage ca. 1840-1844 (before Patrick's ~1845 birth). The most likely repository is a Catholic parish marriage register, given the Irish Catholic community. The FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' is indexed and covers the period. The marriage record (a) supplies the mother's maiden name, which census and death records almost never record; (b) confirms Thomas and his wife as a married couple before Patrick's birth; and (c) by its date, corroborates Thomas as father of a child born ~1845. Important caveat: Thomas may have married in Ireland before emigrating, in which case this search will yield nothing — but the negative result is itself evidence worth recording.

Patrick Flynn was born ~1845 in Ireland, so his own baptism record is in Ireland and not accessible here. However, any younger siblings born after the family arrived in Pennsylvania would have been baptized in a local Catholic parish, with both parents named in the baptismal entry. A sibling's baptism record naming 'Thomas Flynn and [wife's name]' as parents is direct evidence that corroborates the parentage hypothesis and simultaneously reveals the mother's maiden name. items.rationale: Patrick Flynn died in 1908 in Schuylkill County — within Pennsylvania's statewide death registration period (began 1906). The FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906-1968' is indexed and freely accessible. The certificate's 'parents' field, if completed by a knowledgeable informant (surviving spouse or child), directly names both father and mother — making this the highest-yield single source for the parentage question. A blank or uncertain parents field is itself a negative data point worth recording.

The 1850 census placed Patrick Flynn (age 5) in Thomas Flynn's household — indirect evidence only, since 1850 does not state relationships. Tracking this household across subsequent censuses substantially strengthens or weakens the parentage hypothesis. In 1860 Patrick (~15) was likely still at home; the 1860 return will also show any wife of Thomas Flynn by name (the 1850 household notes do not record a wife, or her name was not captured). The 1880 census is particularly important: it introduced the relationship-to-head column, which would state 'son' or 'father' explicitly. The 1900 census (Patrick ~55) may give his parents' birthplaces. All four decades are indexed on FamilySearch at no cost. Note: the 1890 census is largely destroyed — bridge between 1880 and 1900.

If Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) died in Schuylkill County, his will or administration in the county Orphans' Court would name his heirs. A will that names 'my son Patrick Flynn' is direct evidence of the parent-child relationship — the strongest possible corroboration for the 1850 census co-enumeration hypothesis. The FamilySearch collection 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' covers Schuylkill County and is indexed. Search under Thomas Flynn; also search variant spellings (Flyn, Flinn). Thomas could have died anytime from the 1860s onward, so the full range 1860-1910 should be checked.

Fallback for the indexed probate search (sequence 3). FamilySearch volume 007936749 contains 412 browse-only probate images for Schuylkill County, 1851-1930, with 0% indexed. If Thomas Flynn does not surface in the indexed search, these images must be browsed manually — likely organized by decedent surname within the county docket books. Browse this volume if the indexed search returns no result.

A dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn and his wife is required in every parentage plan. Pennsylvania statewide civil marriage registration did not begin until 1913; the county marriage bond/license series for Schuylkill County starts too late for a marriage ca. 1840-1844 (before Patrick's ~1845 birth). dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The plan is factually accurate. All record types, jurisdictions, date ranges, and repository information are correct for Schuylkill County, PA. The skill correctly identified that PA death certificates began 1906 (Patrick died 1908), that the 1880 census introduced relationship-to-head column, that the Diocese of Scranton was formed 1868, and that pre-1906 naturalizations occurred in county courts. All claims are supported by the survey tool responses (wiki_place_page, collections_search, volume_search). No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements. It created the first plan for q_001 (parentage), conducted a full locality survey before planning, cited evidence from the survey (PA death certs 1906+, indexed probate/church collections, browse-only volumes, Diocese of Scranton post-1868), included rationales explaining why each record type was chosen for the parentage question, and included post-1850 census items (1860-1900, with explicit mention of 1880 relationship column). All 9 plan items were written with detailed rationales.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed correct arguments matching expected_args. place_search used 'Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania' (matches ~Schuylkill); collections_search and volume_search used 'Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States' with correct date range 1845-1910; wiki_place_page called twice with 'Pennsylvania, United States' and sections 'research_tips' and 'online_records' (both match ~Pennsylvania); external_links_search used correct standardPlace; research_append wrote all 9 plan items with correct structure. No fixture_not_found errors. All calls succeeded.

dimensions.name: Record type selection
dimensions.rationale: Every plan item's record_type matches the parentage question's information needs. Vital record (death cert) directly names parents; census tracks household across decades and 1880 shows relationship explicitly; probate names heirs/son; church records supply mother's maiden name and sibling baptisms; naturalization may list wife/children; FAN cluster corroborates family network. Each rationale explains specifically what information the record type provides (e.g., 'certificate's parents field directly names both father and mother'). No generic rationales.

dimensions.name: Sequencing logic
dimensions.rationale: Sequence reflects defensible search order: vital record (highest-yield single source) first; then census (free/indexed, broad household tracking); then probate (indexed, direct evidence if will names son); then fallback to browse-only probate; then church marriage (indexed); then church siblings (indexed); then fallback to browse-only church; then naturalization (browse-only); then FAN cluster (census). Free/indexed sources precede paid/unindexed. Fallback chains explicit: pli_007 fallback_for pli_006, pli_010 fallback_for pli_009. Rationales explain placement logic.

dimensions.name: Jurisdiction accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All jurisdictions are historically accurate for the 1845-1910 period. Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania existed throughout (formed 1811 per place_search response). Diocese of Scranton correctly noted as post-1868 for Catholic records; Diocese of Philadelphia pre-1868. County Orphans' Court for probate is correct. County courts for pre-1906 naturalizations is correct. No anachronistic jurisdictions or boundary errors.

## Research Plan pl_003 — q_001: Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn?

**Context:** `pl_002` (the first plan for this question) is complete. It established Thomas Flynn as probable father through 1860 census co-residence and a secondary death certificate statement, but left three critical gaps: the 1870–1900 censuses are unsearched, the mother's identity is entirely unknown, and Thomas Flynn's probate was not found. This plan targets those gaps.

---

### Execution order

| # | ID | Record type | Jurisdiction | Period | Repository | Notes |
|---|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| 1 | pli_007 | Census | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1880 | FamilySearch | **Highest priority** — first census with explicit relationships; will name Thomas's wife |
| 2 | pli_008 | Census | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1870 | FamilySearch | Fills 10-year gap; Thomas's wife named with age and birthplace |
| 3 | pli_009 | Census | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1900 | FamilySearch | Patrick's own household; records year of immigration |
| 4 | pli_010 | Vital record (marriage) | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1840–1875 | FamilySearch | **Mandatory dedicated item** — Thomas Flynn's marriage; mother's maiden name; col. 1571957 + 1401638 |
| 5 | pli_011 | Church | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1845–1875 | FamilySearch | Catholic parish baptisms (col. 1401638); siblings' baptisms name both parents directly |
| 6 | pli_012 | Church | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1845–1875 | Ancestry | *Fallback for pli_011* — additional PA Catholic registers not in FamilySearch |
| 7 | pli_013 | Vital record (marriage) | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1865–1905 | FamilySearch | Patrick's own marriage may name parents; col. 1921317 + 1571957 |
| 8 | pli_014 | Court (naturalization) | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1845–1880 | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn's naturalization papers; may give Irish county of origin and family members |
| 9 | pli_015 | Census (FAN) | Schuylkill Co., PA | 1860–1880 | FamilySearch | BCG Std. 14 — adjacent Flynn households; co-migrant families as corroborating witnesses |

---

### Key rationales

**pli_007 first** — the 1880 census is uniquely valuable because it's the earliest federal census to state household relationships explicitly. It could advance the parentage conclusion from "probable" to "proved" in a single record if Patrick appears in Thomas Flynn's household as "son."

**pli_010 (Thomas Flynn's marriage) is mandatory** — the mother is completely absent from all records found to date. Important caveat in the rationale: because Patrick was born in Ireland ca. 1845, the parents likely married in Ireland before emigrating. Pennsylvania civil and church records should be searched first to rule out a post-emigration Pennsylvania marriage; if nothing is found, the plan will need to extend to pre-1864 Irish Catholic parish registers.

**pli_012 is a fallback** — search only if pli_011 (FamilySearch Catholic records) returns nothing.

9 searches (8 primary + 1 conditional fallback). Ready to execute — would you like to start with the 1880 census search?

entry.rationale: The 1880 census is the first federal census to explicitly record household relationships (son, daughter, wife, etc.), making it the highest-priority next search. Patrick (~35) may still be in or near Thomas Flynn's household; even if in his own home, Thomas's entry (~62) would name his wife — Patrick's mother — with her relationship and age. A stated 'father/son' relationship in 1880 would directly corroborate the parentage inferred from co-residence in the 1850 and 1860 censuses and would identify the mother, who is absent from every record found to date.

Fills the 10-year gap between the confirmed 1860 enumeration and the 1880 census. Patrick (~25) likely remained in or near Thomas Flynn's household during this decade of peak anthracite-coal growth in Schuylkill County. Thomas's wife (Patrick's mother) is named in the 1870 census with age and birthplace — facts absent from all current assertions. The 1870 census also records real estate and personal property values, which help distinguish the correct Thomas Flynn from others of the same name. Search both for Patrick's own entry and for Thomas Flynn's full household.

Patrick (~55) likely heads his own household by 1900. The 1900 census uniquely records year of immigration and number of years in the United States for every foreign-born person — data that would corroborate the ca. 1845 Irish birth and help pin down when Thomas Flynn's family emigrated. Patrick's siblings, if still in Schuylkill County, may appear in adjacent households; sibling entries that name common parents in their own death or church records constitute FAN evidence for the parentage conclusion.

A dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn and his wife is required in every parentage plan. The marriage record would: (a) confirm the couple as a unit; (b) supply the mother's maiden name, which is absent from every record found to date; and (c) by its date relative to Patrick's ca. 1845 Irish birth, corroborate Thomas as parent. Important caveat: because Patrick was born in Ireland ca. 1845, the parents likely married in Ireland before emigrating — a Pennsylvania civil or church marriage may not exist. Search FamilySearch Pennsylvania Civil Marriages (col. 1571957, 1677–1950) and the Pennsylvania Church and Town Records (col. 1401638) first to rule out a Pennsylvania marriage. If no Pennsylvania record is found, a separate Ireland-focused search targeting pre-1864 Catholic parish registers will be required.

Irish immigrants in the Schuylkill County anthracite coal region were overwhelmingly Roman Catholic (Diocese of Harrisburg, later Diocese of Allentown). Catholic parish registers — baptism, marriage, and burial — predate civil vital registration in Pennsylvania and routinely name both parents in baptism entries. A baptism record for Patrick or any sibling in a Schuylkill County parish (e.g., St. Patrick's Pottsville, St. Mary's Shenandoah) would be direct parentage evidence naming both father and mother. FamilySearch's Pennsylvania Church and Town Records (col. 1401638) contains digitized Catholic registers for this region and is the primary access point.

Fallback to the FamilySearch Pennsylvania Church and Town Records search (pli_011). Ancestry holds additional digitized Pennsylvania Catholic registers not included in the FamilySearch collection, including some diocese archives and retrospective parish compilations. If FamilySearch yields no baptism for Patrick or his siblings in Schuylkill County parishes, Ancestry's Catholic records should be searched before concluding that no Pennsylvania church record exists.

Patrick Flynn's own marriage record may name his parents — a practice common in the latter nineteenth century on Pennsylvania marriage licenses and returns. FamilySearch holds Pennsylvania County Marriages (col. 1921317, 1885–1950) and Pennsylvania Civil Marriages (col. 1571957, 1677–1950), both covering Schuylkill County. Knowing Patrick's wife also enables a FAN pivot: her family's obituaries and death certificates sometimes mention in-laws by name and origin, providing additional corroboration of Thomas Flynn's parentage.

Irish immigrants of Thomas Flynn's era (arriving likely ca. 1845–1849) naturalized in their county's Court of Common Pleas. Thomas Flynn's declaration of intent and final naturalization papers would give his approximate age, county of birth in Ireland, and sometimes names of his wife and children. Pre-1906 Pennsylvania naturalizations are in county court records; FamilySearch holds digitized Schuylkill County court records. Establishing Thomas Flynn's Irish county of origin would also support a follow-on search in Ireland for the family's pre-emigration marriage and Patrick's baptism.

BCG Standard 14 requires at least one search targeting the family, associates, and neighbors of the subject. Other Flynn households enumerated near Thomas Flynn in the 1860 and 1880 censuses may include his siblings or cousins; co-migrant Irish families who share naming patterns (e.g., a son named Patrick) or the same Irish townland in naturalization papers can corroborate or extend the parentage evidence. The neighbor enumeration pages adjacent to Thomas Flynn's dwelling in both censuses should be reviewed, and any other Flynn household heads in Schuylkill County cross-checked against Thomas's known facts (age, birthplace, wife's name once identified).

**Subject:** Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg, b. 20 Aug 1779, Stagstrup Sogn, Thisted Amt, Denmark

Try the name index first; the collection is partially indexed before falling back to image browse (step 4).

The 1787 Danish census (Denmark Census, 1787–1930, col. 1401625) is fully indexed and records household composition with ages and relationships. Mikkel would be ~8 years old and almost certainly living with his parents in Stagstrup. A hit names the father directly and provides FAN context — also check the Dansk Demografisk Database transcription at ddd.dda.dk as a free supplement.

Denmark, Military Levying Rolls (Lægdsruller), 1789–1861 (col. 2513529), image group 004748896 (Vestervig/Thisted Amt districts, 1789–1814, 1,204 images, 0% indexed). Mikkel (b. 1779) would be enrolled near the first session. The father's name is written directly above the son's entry — direct parentage evidence ranking alongside the baptism. Must be browsed; also freely browsable via Rigsarkivet at sa.dk/ao.

Stagstrup parish register image group 004130616 (1763–1814, 486 images, only 3% indexed). If the step 1 index search misses the August 1779 baptism, browse these images directly to locate the entry naming Mikkel's father. Also freely browsable at Rigsarkivet (sa.dk/ao — Kirkebøger, Thisted Amt).

Dedicated search for the parents' marriage in the same Stagstrup parish register (image group 004130616, 1763–1814). Mikkel was born 20 Aug 1779, so the couple likely married ca. 1770–1778. **Starting-point check (BCG Standard 11):** Thomas Flynn as candidate father rests on single census co-enumeration (h_001, indirect evidence only). No mother identified. Patrick's birthplace is **Ireland** per source assertion a_002 — the project objective says "Pennsylvania," which is unsupported and should be noted. The plan accounts for these gaps.

**Locality survey findings:**
- Schuylkill County formed 1811; no relevant boundary changes
- PA statewide death registration: **1906** → Patrick's 1908 death cert should exist
- Irish Catholic community → Diocese of Philadelphia (pre-1868) / Diocese of Harrisburg (1868+)
- FamilySearch has: Church & Town Records 1708-1985 (indexed, col. 1401638) and Probate Records 1683-1994 (browse-only images, col. 1417683)
- Coal-mining community → Civil War service likely; Patrick was ~16-20 during the warPlan **pl_002** written successfully — 9 items, all validated. Here's what was created:

---

## Research Plan pl_002 — *Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn?* (q_001)

**Starting-point note (BCG Standard 11):** The only evidence linking Patrick to Thomas Flynn is 1850 census co-enumeration (indirect, hypothesis h_001). No mother has been identified. The 1850 source says Patrick was born in **Ireland** — the project objective's "Pennsylvania" is unsupported and should not be relied upon.

---

### Item 1 — pli_004 · Patrick's 1908 death certificate · FamilySearch · *vital_record*
Pennsylvania statewide death registration began in 1906; Patrick's 1908 death cert should exist and routinely requested parents' names and birthplaces. If the informant had personal knowledge, this record names both parents outright — including the mother's maiden name no census supplies. **Highest probability item; search first.**

---

### Item 2 — pli_005 · 1880 census · FamilySearch · *census*
The 1880 census introduced the relationship-to-head column — the **first census to explicitly state family relationships**. Thomas Flynn (~62 in 1880) may still be living; his household would carry "son"/"daughter" for any remaining children. Search for both Thomas and Patrick Flynn in Schuylkill County.

---

### Item 3 — pli_006 · 1860 census · FamilySearch · *census*
Patrick (~15 in 1860) is likely still in Thomas's household. The 1860 census names all members but doesn't state relationships; its primary value here is identifying **Thomas Flynn's wife by name** — the candidate mother who hasn't been extracted from any source yet. It also reveals siblings who enable item 9.

---

### Item 4 — pli_007 · Thomas Flynn probate · FamilySearch · *probate*
FamilySearch collection 1417683 (Pennsylvania Probate Records, 1683-1994) — browse-only images. A will naming Patrick as a son is direct parentage evidence. Thomas (born ~1818) would have died 1860–1910. Schuylkill County Orphans' Court records survive well with no major courthouse fire.

---

### Item 5 — pli_008 · Thomas Flynn's marriage to his wife · FamilySearch · *church*
**BCG-required dedicated item for the candidate parents' marriage.** Confirms Thomas and the mother as a married couple; supplies the **mother's maiden name**; and by its date relative to Patrick's ~1845 birth, corroborates the census co-enumeration. Irish Catholic records are partially indexed in FamilySearch collection 1401638 (Pennsylvania Church and Town Records, 1708-1985). *Note: if the couple married in Ireland before emigrating, follow-up Irish church searches will be needed.*

---

### Item 6 — pli_009 · Patrick Flynn's marriage · FamilySearch · *church*
Catholic marriage registers routinely entered the groom's parents. Patrick (born ~1845) likely married 1865–1885 in Schuylkill County. A register entry naming Thomas Flynn and his wife as Patrick's parents would be contemporaneous direct evidence, independent of the 1850 census. Same collection 1401638.

---

### Item 7 — pli_010 · Civil War military / pension · FamilySearch · *military*
Patrick was 16–20 during the Civil War; Schuylkill County's Irish coal-mining community was heavily enlisted in Pennsylvania regiments. A widow's pension filed after Patrick's 1908 death could contain affidavits naming his parents — pension files are among the richest genealogical sources for this era.

---

### Item 8 — pli_011 · Irish passenger lists 1845–1850 · FamilySearch · *immigration*
The family emigrated from Ireland during peak Famine years (1845–1850). If Thomas Flynn traveled with his wife and infant Patrick as a family group, the passenger manifest names them together — providing the mother's name and corroborating that Patrick arrived with Thomas. Search New York, Philadelphia, and Boston port arrivals.

---

### Item 9 — pli_012 · FAN cluster — siblings' death certificates · FamilySearch · *vital_record* (contingent on item 3)
*Per BCG Standard 14 — at least one FAN-directed search.* Once the 1860 census (item 3) identifies Patrick's siblings from Thomas's household, search their post-1906 death certificates on FamilySearch. A sibling's death cert independently naming Thomas Flynn and wife as parents corroborates any statement found in Patrick's own certificate, building preponderance of evidence.

---

|---|---|
| Total searches | 9 |
| Free (FamilySearch) | 9 of 9 |
| Browse-only | 1 (pli_007, probate) |
| Contingent | 1 (pli_012, depends on item 3) |

entry.rationale: Patrick died in 1908 in Schuylkill County, two years after Pennsylvania began statewide death registration (1906). Death certificates of this era routinely requested the decedent's parents' names and birthplaces. If the informant — typically a spouse, child, or sibling with personal knowledge — knew Patrick's parents, this record would directly name both father and mother and supply the mother's maiden name, which no census record provides. This is the single record most likely to answer the question outright. Search FamilySearch's indexed Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906-1966.

The 1880 federal census introduced the relationship-to-head column — the first census to explicitly state family relationships rather than leaving them to inference from household position. Thomas Flynn (born ~1818) would be ~62 in 1880 and may still be living. His household would list any children still at home as 'son' or 'daughter,' providing the first direct census statement of parentage. Patrick (born ~1845, age ~35) likely has his own household by 1880 but may live nearby. Search for both Thomas Flynn and Patrick Flynn in Schuylkill County; Thomas's household is the primary target for the relationship column.

Patrick would be approximately 15 in 1860 and is likely still in Thomas Flynn's household. Crucially, the 1860 census names all household members but does not record relationships — meaning this search's primary value is identifying Thomas Flynn's wife (the candidate mother), who was not extracted from the 1850 record. The wife's name in the 1860 household is the first traceable lead to the mother's identity. The search also reveals any siblings by name and age, enabling the FAN cluster search in item 9 (pli_012).

| pli_011 | Civil War service/pension records | military | 1861–1865 | FamilySearch | Thomas born ~1818 was 43–47 during the war — eligible for PA Emergency Militia. A pension file could name widow and children, directly bearing on parentage. |
| pli_012 | Cemetery records (FindAGrave) | cemetery | 1860–1870 | other | *Fallback for pli_009.* If church registers yield nothing, a tombstone inscription provides death date and may show a family plot with Patrick. |

**Fallback relationship:** pli_012 → fallback for pli_009 (church records).  
**Scope:** 6 searches.

### One note on a potential conflict
The existing sources include a *will dated 1881* (src_006) and a *deed "Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn, 1881"* (src_008). If Thomas died ~1865, these 1881 records most likely represent the **probate year** (wills are often probated years after death) and an **executor/estate deed** — not evidence Thomas was alive in 1881. Worth keeping in mind as you work through pli_007; the probate file itself should clarify.

---

**Ready to start?** The natural first step is **pli_007** — the Schuylkill County probate/administration browse on FamilySearch (collection 1417683). Say "execute the plan" or "search probate records" and the search-records skill will pick it up.

args.query: Pennsylvania probate estate administration records 1860s genealogy

entry.rationale: New evidence places Thomas Flynn's death ca. 1865, within this window. The Schuylkill County Register of Wills holds wills and letters of administration; a will or intestate administration file naming Patrick as heir is direct evidence of parentage. FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' (collection 1417683) covers this period as browse-only images — browse by county and year. Supersedes pli_006 (date range corrected from 1870-1890 to 1860-1870).

If Thomas Flynn died ca. 1865, he should be absent from the 1870 census — his absence corroborates the new death-date reference. Searching for his widow and surviving household members may surface a widow's household with children still at home, adding further evidence bearing on parentage and the family's post-1865 circumstances.

Pennsylvania had no statewide death registration until 1906; church registers are the primary pre-registration death-date source. St. Mary's Catholic Church, Pottsville, already appears in this project (Patrick's baptism, src_005). A burial or death entry for Thomas Flynn would supply his death date and may name survivors. Search FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985' (collection 1401638).

Pennsylvania county tax assessments were compiled annually. Thomas Flynn's last appearance in the assessment rolls and his first year of absence bracket his death year, providing independent corroboration of the ca. 1865 date. FamilySearch holds some Pennsylvania county tax records; the Schuylkill County courthouse holds originals if not digitized.

Thomas Flynn born ca. 1818 was approximately 43–47 during the Civil War — within the age range for Pennsylvania Emergency Militia enrollment. A death in military service or shortly after discharge would account for the ca. 1865 date. FamilySearch holds Pennsylvania Civil War compiled service records and pension indexes; a pension file could name widow and children, directly bearing on parentage.

Fallback for church death records (pli_009): if St. Mary's or other local church registers yield no entry, a cemetery inscription is the next-best physical record of Thomas Flynn's death date and burial location. FindAGrave has extensive Pennsylvania coverage. A family plot shared with Patrick or a stone noting survivors would further corroborate parentage.

All persisted values are factually correct: probate/church/tax/military/cemetery records are appropriate for death-date research in Pennsylvania pre-1906, jurisdiction is accurate (Schuylkill County), and the rationale correctly notes Pennsylvania had no statewide death registration until 1906. The note about the 1881 will and deed as likely probate/executor records is a sound caveat. No fabricated material or unsupported claims.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: superseded the prior plan without modifying its items (preserving audit trail), created a new plan with 6 items covering the 1860-1870 window, included FAN-adjacent items (church, tax, military records to corroborate death), maintained free-before-paid sequencing (FamilySearch collections first, FindAGrave fallback last), identified a fallback chain (pli_012 → pli_009), and provided explicit rationales explaining why each record type was chosen. The user's request to revise the plan for the 1860-1870 period was fully addressed.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All five MCP tool calls passed arguments matching expected_args semantically. place_search used 'Schuylkill County' (matches ~Schuylkill); wiki_search queried probate records (matches ~probate); collections_search passed standardPlace 'Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States' with 1860-1870 date range (matches ~Schuylkill); external_links_search passed the same standardPlace (matches ~); research_append calls used correct section/op/entryId/fields for superseding pl_002 and appending pl_003 with 6 items. No fixture_not_found errors. All critical identifiers (plan IDs, item IDs, collection IDs) are correct.

dimensions.name: Record type selection
dimensions.rationale: Every plan item's record_type matches the research question's information need. Probate (pli_007) targets parentage via will/administration naming Patrick as heir; census (pli_008) corroborates death absence; church (pli_009) provides death date (primary source pre-1906 registration); tax (pli_010) brackets death year via appearance/absence; military (pli_011) addresses Civil War service and pension records naming survivors; cemetery (pli_012) is a fallback for death date. Each rationale explicitly names what information the record type provides (e.g., 'probate file naming Patrick as heir is direct evidence of parentage'; 'church registers are the primary pre-registration death-date source'). No generic rationales.

dimensions.name: Sequencing logic
dimensions.rationale: Sequence numbers reflect defensible search order: free/indexed FamilySearch collections (pli_007–pli_011) before paid/external (pli_012 FindAGrave). Broad searches (probate, census, church) precede narrow (tax, military). Fallback chain is explicit: pli_012 fallback_for='pli_009' (cemetery as backup if church records fail). Each item's rationale explains its placement (e.g., pli_007 is first because probate directly names heirs; pli_008 follows to corroborate; pli_009 is primary death source; pli_012 is fallback). Logic is clear and defensible.

Military records are correctly scoped to Pennsylvania (state-level Civil War service). No anachronistic or non-existent jurisdictions are named.
